name: Umm Mahjan
name_lines: The Woman Who Cleaned the Masjid
epithet: The Woman Who Cleaned the Masjid
title: Umm Mahjan (RA): The Woman Who Cleaned the Masjid
meta_description: The story of Umm Mahjan (RA), the freed African woman who quietly cleaned and perfumed the Prophet's masjid in Madinah. When she died, the Prophet Muhammad noticed, and prayed at her grave.
keywords: Umm Mahjan, woman who cleaned the masjid, sahaba stories, the firsts, freed slave companion, cleaning the mosque, sahabiyat, the unnoticed companions
lede: Some of the companions are remembered for great battles or famous words. This one is remembered for a broom, a little tent in the corner of the masjid, and the scent she left behind after everyone else had gone home.

This is the story of Umm Mahjan (may Allah be pleased with her): a freed woman whose whole biography is short, but whom the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ noticed, missed, and honoured in a way that still stops people in their tracks.

paras: There is a kind of person who disappears in a crowded community. They come and they go, and no one asks about them. No one says salam. They are the people most easily forgotten, and most easily taken advantage of, and the more involved the rest of us are in the affairs of the community, the easier they are to miss.

Umm Mahjan was one of those people. We do not know her tribe. We do not know her father. We have only her first name, and a handful of narrations that have to be pieced together carefully, sometimes guessing whether one story is even about the same woman. Her biography is short. Most of what survives is a thin thread of who she was and how she lived.

What we do know is that the Prophet ﷺ did not miss her. Somehow, in a city he was building from nothing, with people arriving from every direction and learning a new life almost overnight, he saw the very ones everyone else walked past. He noticed them, and he gave them a stature far above their station in the eyes of the world. The two or three things we know about Umm Mahjan are enough to make a person love him all over again.

sections.h2: A house of Allah, kept clean
paras: The Prophet ﷺ taught the believers that wherever they settled, the masjid came first. Build it, he told them, and then take care of it. Keep it clean. Keep it fragrant. As Islam spread, people would raise the masjid of a place before they raised their own homes, so that a whole neighbourhood grew up with the same mindset: the centre of our life is here, and its upkeep is everyone's responsibility.

He did not leave this to others. He was seen cleaning the masjid with his own hands, even peeling away something dried that a new convert had wiped onto his pulpit. People were arriving in waves, from every background, some of whom only yesterday had no idea there was an unseen God at all. He taught them gently, and patiently, that this was the house of Allah and was to be treated as such.

Once he saw a stain on the wall of the masjid and his face reddened with displeasure. A woman rose, scraped it away herself, cleaned the spot, and put perfume in its place. His face softened at once, and he praised what she had done, calling it a beautiful act. We are told it may well have been her.

sections.h2: The little tent in the corner
paras: Umm Mahjan lived in the masjid itself. She had a small hut, or a sturdy tent of leather, the kind made not for a short journey but to last, pitched in a corner of it. When the prayer ended and the rows emptied and everyone returned to their homes, she would go quietly around the space, clean it, and perfume it for the next gathering. This was her work, unasked for and unannounced, done after the building had emptied out.

Our mother Aisha (RA) used to sit with her. The wife of the Prophet ﷺ, in the house that adjoined the masjid, would come and talk with the woman who swept its floor, and she said plainly that she enjoyed her company. Picture it: after the crowd has gone, sometimes late at night, two women sitting in a quiet corner, talking.

It tells you something about the community the Prophet ﷺ was building. The barriers people had carried their whole lives were quietly coming down. The love, the reverence, the sense that everyone belonged: a forgotten woman with a broom was not beneath the notice of anyone, least of all him.

sections.h2: The day of the red scarf
paras: Aisha (RA) noticed that Umm Mahjan kept humming the same lines of poetry as she worked: that the day of the red scarf was a wonder from her Lord, and that He had saved her from a people who did not believe. One day she asked her what it meant, and the woman sat her down and told her story.

She had been enslaved among a powerful tribe, set to care for the chief's daughter. On a journey, the girl slept with a precious red scarf beside her, and a bird swooped down and carried it off, perhaps mistaking it for food. When the girl woke and cried out, the blame fell on the one who was supposed to be watching. Her master refused to believe that a bird had taken it. They stripped her, beat her, and searched her, and she had done nothing.

While they were still at it, the bird returned and dropped the red scarf down between them. Her master was so shaken by what he had done to her that he set her free. Her freedom came out of the very moment of her suffering. Later she heard that a Prophet had appeared, and curious about what was happening, she came to Madinah, met him ﷺ, and believed. That, she said, was why she sang of the day the red scarf saved her from a people who did not believe.

sections.h2: Honoured by piety, not by lineage
paras: Think of the contrast. There was another woman remembered in revelation only by her husband, named as the one who would carry the firewood of the Fire: the wife of Abu Lahab, who used to scatter filth in the Prophet's path. Wealth and standing did not save her.

And here is Umm Mahjan, from the humblest of beginnings, a freed slave whose family no one recorded. She does not carry firewood. She becomes royalty in the words of the Prophet ﷺ, counted among the most honoured generation that has ever lived. The world would have placed these two women at opposite ends of every ladder. Revelation reversed the order.

This was the lesson the believers had to learn, and it did not come quickly. Old pride and old tribalism kept resurfacing. To truly accept that this woman with a broom outranked you in the sight of God was hard. The Qur'an had already settled the matter:

ayah.text: People, We created you all from a single man and a single woman, and made you into races and tribes so that you should recognize one another. In God's eyes, the most honoured of you are the ones most mindful of Him: God is all knowing, all aware.

paras: One day the Prophet ﷺ came out and did not see her. In a masjid full of people, he noticed the absence of the one who swept its floor, and he asked where she was. They told him she had died. She had passed in the night, they said, and some of the women had washed her and prayed over her and buried her, and they had not wanted to disturb him at so late an hour.

He was not pleased. Could you not have told me, he asked. He understood at once what had happened: they had thought her affair too small to mention. She was only the woman who cleaned the masjid. Had she been someone of status, there would have been an announcement, a crowd. For her, there had been silence.

So he said, take me to her grave. They led him to it, and he stood and prayed over her again, a prayer of his own. Then he said that these graves are filled with darkness for those in them, and that Allah lights them up by his praying over them. The difference between his prayer and its absence, he was teaching, could be the difference between darkness and light. Never again, he was saying, think anyone is too small to matter.

There is a second account, weaker in its chain and one we cannot confirm, but it carries the same spirit and it is where her name itself surfaces. In it the Prophet ﷺ saw a fresh grave and asked whose it was. When they told him, he recognised her at once, the one who lived in the masjid and cared for it, and he lined the people up to pray over her.

Then, the narration says, he spoke to her, asking which of her deeds she had found most helpful to her now. When the companions wondered whether she could even hear, he reminded them that Allah lets her hear. And she answered that it was the cleaning of the masjid.

Whether or not those exact words were spoken, the whole story bends toward one truth: a small, unglamorous act, done faithfully and without applause, may be the very thing that lights a person's grave. And that is all we know of her, the one who lived in the masjid, cleaned it, perfumed it, and left behind a story far larger than her name.

lessons_heading: What Umm Mahjan's life teaches us
lessons_intro: Her biography is a few lines long, and most of it is a broom and a corner of a mosque. The lessons inside it are not small at all.

lessons.bold: No good deed is too small.
lessons.text: She swept floors and carried perfume, and it raised her to a rank that wealth and lineage could not buy. Faithfulness in a small task is still faithfulness.
The Prophet ﷺ saw the woman everyone else walked past, missed her when she was gone, and corrected those who thought her affair too small to mention.
A freed woman with no recorded family became royalty in his words, while pride and standing saved no one. The most honoured are simply the most mindful of God.
Removing something harmful from a mosque, keeping it clean and fragrant, is a quiet act of love that the Prophet ﷺ praised and never belittled.

faq.a: A freed woman, said to be of African origin, who lived in a small tent in the corner of the Prophet's masjid in Madinah and took it upon herself to clean and perfume it. Little is recorded of her life beyond her first name and a few narrations.

faq.q: Why is she remembered when so little is known about her?
faq.a: When she died and was buried quietly at night, the Prophet ﷺ noticed she was missing, was troubled that no one had told him, and went to her grave to pray over her. His care for an unnoticed woman became one of the most moving lessons in the seerah.

faq.q: What did the Prophet ﷺ say at her grave?
faq.a: He prayed over her and said that these graves are full of darkness for those in them, and that Allah fills them with light through his prayer over them. He was teaching that no believer is too insignificant to honour.

faq.q: What can we learn from Umm Mahjan?
faq.a: That no good deed is too small, that we should notice the people others overlook, and that true honour comes from piety rather than status or lineage.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to measure ourselves by the things that get noticed: titles, audiences, the work that earns thanks. Umm Mahjan did none of that. She cleaned an empty room after everyone left, and it turned out to be the deed that lit her grave. Her story quietly asks what we are doing when no one is watching, and whether we would still do it if no one ever clapped. The next time you pass a piece of litter on the floor of a mosque, or anywhere, you may find you cannot walk by it the same way again.

It also asks something harder. There is almost certainly an Umm Mahjan near you: someone who comes and goes and is never asked about, in your masjid, your workplace, your street. The Prophet ﷺ noticed her, missed her, and refused to let her be buried in silence. Carry a little of that into today. Greet the overlooked person. Honour the small act. Believe, and act as though you believe, that in the sight of God the quietest servant may be the most honoured of all.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umm Mahjan (RA), the woman who cleaned the masjid, in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Qur'an translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 49:13). Only her first name is preserved in the records, and some details are pieced together from separate narrations, including one weaker account noted as such in the telling.

slides:
Some companions are remembered for great battles.

This one is remembered for a broom, a little tent in the corner of the masjid, and the scent she left behind after everyone went home.

We do not know her tribe.

We do not know her father.

We have only her first name: Umm Mahjan.

The Prophet ﷺ knew her well.

She lived in a small tent inside the masjid.

When the prayer ended and the rows emptied, she would quietly clean the floor and perfume it for the next gathering.

Our mother Aisha (RA) used to sit and talk with her late into the evening.

The barriers people carried their whole lives were quietly coming down.

She had been enslaved, beaten for a theft she did not commit.

Then the truth came back on its own, she was freed, she heard of the Prophet ﷺ, and she believed.

One day he came out and did not see her.

"Where is she?"

They said she had died in the night, and they had not wanted to disturb him.

He was troubled. They had thought her affair too small to mention.

"Take me to her grave."

He stood and prayed over her himself.

**No good deed is too small.**

She swept an empty room when no one was watching, and it lit her grave.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
Some of the companions are remembered for battles and famous words. Umm Mahjan (may Allah be pleased with her) is remembered for a broom.

A freed woman whose family no one recorded, she lived in a small tent in the corner of the Prophet's masjid and cleaned and perfumed it after everyone had gone home. When she died and was buried quietly at night, the Prophet ﷺ noticed she was missing, was troubled that no one had told him, and went to her grave to pray over her.

No deed is too small. No person is too easy to overlook. 🤍

Save this, and share it with someone who needs it today.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/umm-mahjan

email.subject: The companion remembered for a broom
email.alt_subject: The Prophet noticed she was gone
email.preview: She cleaned the masjid after everyone left. When she died, the Prophet went to her grave.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Some of the companions are remembered for great battles or famous words. Today's is remembered for something much quieter: a broom, and a little tent in the corner of the masjid.

Her name was Umm Mahjan (may Allah be pleased with her). We do not know her tribe or her father. We have only her first name, and a few narrations pieced together. She had been enslaved, once beaten for a theft she did not commit, then freed when the truth came out on its own. She heard that a Prophet had appeared, came to Madinah, and believed.

In Madinah she lived in the masjid itself, in a small tent in the corner. When the prayer ended and everyone went home, she would quietly clean the floor and perfume it for the next gathering. Our mother Aisha (RA) used to sit and talk with her late into the evening, and said she enjoyed her company.

Then one day the Prophet ﷺ came out and did not see her. He asked where she was. They told him she had died in the night, and that they had not wanted to disturb him, so some of the women had washed her, prayed over her, and buried her.

He was not pleased. Could you not have told me, he asked. He understood what had happened: they had thought her affair too small to mention. She was only the woman who cleaned the masjid.

So he said, take me to her grave. He stood and prayed over her himself, and said that these graves are full of darkness for those in them, and that Allah fills them with light through his prayer.

There is a lesson here that is easy to miss. No good deed is too small, and no person is too easy to overlook. She swept an empty room when no one was watching, and it lit her grave. The Prophet ﷺ noticed the woman everyone else walked past, and refused to let her be buried in silence.

May Allah be pleased with her, and teach us to see the people we so easily miss.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Some of the companions are remembered for great battles or famous words. This one is remembered for a broom, a little tent in the corner of the masjid, and the scent she left behind after everyone else had gone home.

Her name was Umm Mahjan (may Allah be pleased with her). We do not know her tribe or her father. She had been enslaved among a powerful people, and once, on a journey, a bird carried off a precious red scarf and she was blamed and beaten for a theft she had not committed. When the bird dropped the scarf back between them, her shaken master set her free. Later she heard that a Prophet had appeared, came to Madinah, and believed.

In Madinah she lived in the masjid itself, in a small hut pitched in a corner. When the prayer ended and the rows emptied and everyone returned home, she would go quietly around the space, clean it, and perfume it for the next gathering.

Our mother Aisha (RA) used to sit with her and talk late into the evening, and said plainly that she enjoyed her company. The barriers people had carried their whole lives were quietly coming down, and a forgotten woman with a broom was beneath the notice of no one.

Then one day the Prophet ﷺ came out and did not see her. He asked where she was. They told him she had died in the night, and that they had not wanted to disturb him, so the women had washed her and prayed over her and buried her. He was troubled. Could you not have told me, he asked. They had thought her affair too small to mention.

So he said, take me to her grave. They led him to it, and he stood and prayed over her himself, and said that these graves are full of darkness for those in them, and that Allah fills them with light through his prayer over them.
She swept an empty room when no one was watching, and it lit her grave.
storybook.reflection: Umm Mahjan did the work that no one applauds: cleaning an empty room after everyone left. It turned out to be the deed that lit her grave. Her story asks what we do when no one is watching, and whether we notice the quiet people the way the Prophet ﷺ noticed her. No good deed is too small, and no person is too easy to overlook.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
